`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-1) sequenceDiagram [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-2)     participant Client [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-3)     participant Server [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-4)     participant Crawler [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-5) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-6)     Client->>Server: Start crawl request [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-7)     Server->>Crawler: Initiate crawl with Process ID [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-8)     Crawler-->>Server: Event: Page hit [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-9)     Server-->>Client: Stream: Page hit event [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-10)     Client->>Server: Instruction for Process ID [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-11)     Server->>Crawler: Update crawl with new instructions [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-12)     Crawler-->>Server: Event: Crawl completed [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/dockerize_hooks/#__codelineno-0-13)     Server-->>Client: Stream: Crawl completed`

**Benefits for Developers and Users**

1. **Fine-Grained Control**: Instead of predefining all logic upfront, you can dynamically guide the crawler in response to actual data and conditions encountered mid-crawl. 2. **Real-Time Insights**: Monitor progress, errors, or network bottlenecks as they happen, without waiting for the entire crawl to finish. 3. **Enhanced Collaboration**: Different team members or automated systems can watch the same crawl events and provide input, making the crawling process more adaptive and intelligent.

**Next Steps**

I’m currently exploring the best APIs, technologies, and patterns to make this vision a reality. My goal is to deliver a seamless developer experience—one that integrates with existing Crawl4AI workflows while offering new flexibility and power.

Stay tuned for more updates as I continue building this feature out. In the meantime, I’d love to hear any feedback or suggestions you might have to help shape this interactive, event-driven future of web crawling with Crawl4AI.

* * *

\> Feedback

---

# digest() - Crawl4AI Documentation (v0.8.x)

digest()
========

The `digest()` method is the primary interface for adaptive web crawling. It intelligently crawls websites starting from a given URL, guided by a query, and automatically determines when sufficient information has been gathered.

Method Signature
----------------

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-0-1) async def digest( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-0-2)     start_url: str, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-0-3)     query: str, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-0-4)     resume_from: Optional[Union[str, Path]] = None [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-0-5) ) -> CrawlState`

Parameters
----------

### start\_url

*   **Type**: `str`
*   **Required**: Yes
*   **Description**: The starting URL for the crawl. This should be a valid HTTP/HTTPS URL that serves as the entry point for information gathering.

### query

*   **Type**: `str`
*   **Required**: Yes
*   **Description**: The search query that guides the crawling process. This should contain key terms related to the information you're seeking. The crawler uses this to evaluate relevance and determine which links to follow.

### resume\_from

*   **Type**: `Optional[Union[str, Path]]`
*   **Default**: `None`
*   **Description**: Path to a previously saved crawl state file. When provided, the crawler resumes from the saved state instead of starting fresh.

Return Value
------------

Returns a `CrawlState` object containing:

*   **crawled\_urls** (`Set[str]`): All URLs that have been crawled
*   **knowledge\_base** (`List[CrawlResult]`): Collection of crawled pages with content
*   **pending\_links** (`List[Link]`): Links discovered but not yet crawled
*   **metrics** (`Dict[str, float]`): Performance and quality metrics
*   **query** (`str`): The original query
*   Additional statistical information for scoring

How It Works
------------

The `digest()` method implements an intelligent crawling algorithm:

1.  **Initial Crawl**: Starts from the provided URL
2.  **Link Analysis**: Evaluates all discovered links for relevance
3.  **Scoring**: Uses three metrics to assess information sufficiency:
4.  **Coverage**: How well the query terms are covered
5.  **Consistency**: Information coherence across pages
6.  **Saturation**: Diminishing returns detection
7.  **Adaptive Selection**: Chooses the most promising links to follow
8.  **Stopping Decision**: Automatically stops when confidence threshold is reached

Examples
--------

### Basic Usage

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-1-1) async with AsyncWebCrawler() as crawler: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-1-2)     adaptive = AdaptiveCrawler(crawler) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-1-3) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-1-4)     state = await adaptive.digest( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-1-5)         start_url="https://docs.python.org/3/", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-1-6)         query="async await context managers" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-1-7)     ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-1-8) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-1-9)     print(f"Crawled {len(state.crawled_urls)} pages") [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-1-10)     print(f"Confidence: {adaptive.confidence:.0%}")`

### With Configuration

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-1) config = AdaptiveConfig( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-2)     confidence_threshold=0.9,  # Require high confidence [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-3)     max_pages=30,             # Allow more pages [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-4)     top_k_links=3             # Follow top 3 links per page [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-5) ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-6) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-7) adaptive = AdaptiveCrawler(crawler, config=config) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-8) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-9) state = await adaptive.digest( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-10)     start_url="https://api.example.com/docs", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-11)     query="authentication endpoints rate limits" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-2-12) )`

### Resuming a Previous Crawl

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-1) # First crawl - may be interrupted [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-2) state1 = await adaptive.digest( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-3)     start_url="https://example.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-4)     query="machine learning algorithms" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-5) ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-6) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-7) # Save state (if not auto-saved) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-8) state1.save("ml_crawl_state.json") [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-9) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-10) # Later, resume from saved state [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-11) state2 = await adaptive.digest( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-12)     start_url="https://example.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-13)     query="machine learning algorithms", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-14)     resume_from="ml_crawl_state.json" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-3-15) )`

### With Progress Monitoring

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-1) state = await adaptive.digest( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-2)     start_url="https://docs.example.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-3)     query="api reference" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-4) ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-5) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-6) # Monitor progress [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-7) print(f"Pages crawled: {len(state.crawled_urls)}") [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-8) print(f"New terms discovered: {state.new_terms_history}") [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-9) print(f"Final confidence: {adaptive.confidence:.2%}") [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-10) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-11) # View detailed statistics [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-4-12) adaptive.print_stats(detailed=True)`

Query Best Practices
--------------------

1.  **Be Specific**: Use descriptive terms that appear in target content
    
    `[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-5-1) # Good [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-5-2) query = "python async context managers implementation" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-5-3) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-5-4) # Too broad [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-5-5) query = "python programming"`
    
2.  **Include Key Terms**: Add technical terms you expect to find
    
    `[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-6-1) query = "oauth2 jwt refresh tokens authorization"`
    
3.  **Multiple Concepts**: Combine related concepts for comprehensive coverage
    
    `[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-7-1) query = "rest api pagination sorting filtering"`
    

Performance Considerations
--------------------------

*   **Initial URL**: Choose a page with good navigation (e.g., documentation index)
*   **Query Length**: 3-8 terms typically work best
*   **Link Density**: Sites with clear navigation crawl more efficiently
*   **Caching**: Enable caching for repeated crawls of the same domain

Error Handling
--------------

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-8-1) try: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-8-2)     state = await adaptive.digest( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-8-3)         start_url="https://example.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-8-4)         query="search terms" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-8-5)     ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-8-6) except Exception as e: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-8-7)     print(f"Crawl failed: {e}") [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/digest/#__codelineno-8-8)     # State is auto-saved if save_state=True in config`

Stopping Conditions
-------------------

The crawl stops when any of these conditions are met:

1.  **Confidence Threshold**: Reached the configured confidence level
2.  **Page Limit**: Crawled the maximum number of pages
3.  **Diminishing Returns**: Expected information gain below threshold
4.  **No Relevant Links**: No promising links remain to follow

See Also
--------

*   [AdaptiveCrawler Class](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/api/adaptive-crawler/)
    
*   [Adaptive Crawling Guide](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/adaptive-crawling/)
    
*   [Configuration Options](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/adaptive-crawling/#configuration-options)
    

* * *

\> Feedback

---

# The LLM Context Protocol: Why Your AI Assistant Needs Memory, Reasoning, and Examples - Crawl4AI Documentation (v0.8.x)

The LLM Context Protocol: Why Your AI Assistant Needs Memory, Reasoning, and Examples
=====================================================================================

_Published on January 24, 2025 • 8 min read_

* * *

The Problem with Teaching Robots to Code
----------------------------------------

Picture this: You hand someone a dictionary and ask them to write poetry. They know every word, its spelling, its definition—but do they know how words dance together? How certain combinations evoke emotion while others fall flat? This is exactly what we're doing when we throw API documentation at our AI assistants and expect magic.

I've spent countless hours watching my AI coding assistant struggle with my own library, Crawl4AI. Despite feeding it comprehensive documentation, it would generate code that was _technically_ correct but practically useless. Like a tourist speaking from a phrasebook—grammatically sound, culturally tone-deaf.

Enter the Three-Dimensional Context Protocol
--------------------------------------------

What if, instead of dumping information, we provided _wisdom_? Not just the "what," but the "how" and "why"? This led me to develop what I call the **LLM Context Protocol**—a structured approach that mirrors how humans actually master libraries.

Think of it as HTTP for AI context. Just as HTTP doesn't dictate your website's content but provides a reliable structure for communication, this protocol doesn't prescribe _how_ you write your documentation—it provides a framework for _what_ your AI needs to truly understand your code.

### The Three Pillars of Library Wisdom

\#### 🧠 \*\*Memory: The Foundation\*\*

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-1) # AsyncWebCrawler.arun() - Memory Context [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-2) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-3) ## Signature [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-4) async def arun( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-5)     url: str, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-6)     config: CrawlerConfig = None, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-7)     session_id: str = None, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-8)     **kwargs [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-9) ) -> CrawlResult [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-10) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-11) ## Parameters [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-12) - url: Target URL to crawl [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-13) - config: Optional configuration object [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-14) - session_id: Optional session identifier for caching [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-0-15) ...`

This is your API reference—the facts, the parameters, the return types. It's the easiest part to generate and, ironically, the least useful in isolation. It's like memorizing a dictionary without understanding grammar.

\#### 🎯 \*\*Reasoning: The Soul\*\*

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-1) # AsyncWebCrawler Design Philosophy - Reasoning Context [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-2) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-3) ## Why Async-First Architecture? [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-4) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-5) Crawl4AI uses AsyncWebCrawler as its primary interface because modern web  [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-6) scraping demands concurrency. Here's the thinking: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-7) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-8) 1. **Network I/O is slow**: Waiting synchronously wastes 90% of execution time [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-9) 2. **Modern sites are complex**: Multiple resources load in parallel [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-10) 3. **Scale matters**: You're rarely crawling just one page [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-11) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-12) ## When to Use Session Management [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-13) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-14) Session management isn't just about performance—it's about appearing human: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-15) - Use sessions when crawling multiple pages from the same domain [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-16) - Reuse browser contexts to maintain cookies and local storage [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-17) - But don't overdo it: too long sessions look suspicious [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-18) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-19) ## The Cache Strategy Decision Tree [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-20) if static_content and infrequent_updates: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-21)     use_cache_mode('read_write') [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-22) elif dynamic_content and real_time_needed: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-23)     use_cache_mode('bypass') [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-24) else: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-1-25)     use_cache_mode('read_only')  # Safe default`

This is where the library creator's philosophy lives. It's not just \*what\* the library does, but \*why\* it does it that way. This is the hardest part to write because it requires genuine understanding—and it's a red flag when a library lacks it.

\#### 💻 \*\*Examples: The Practice\*\*

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-1) # Crawling with JavaScript execution [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-2) result = await crawler.arun( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-3)     url="https://example.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-4)     js_code="window.scrollTo(0, document.body.scrollHeight);", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-5)     wait_for="css:.lazy-loaded-content" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-6) ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-7) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-8) # Extracting structured data with CSS selectors [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-9) result = await crawler.arun( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-10)     url="https://shop.example.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-11)     extraction_strategy=CSSExtractionStrategy({ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-12)         "prices": "span.price::text", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-13)         "titles": "h2.product-title::text" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-14)     }) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-15) ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-16) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-17) # Session-based crawling with custom headers [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-18) async with crawler: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-19)     result1 = await crawler.arun(url1, session_id="product_scan") [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/articles/llm-context-revolution/#__codelineno-2-20)     result2 = await crawler.arun(url2, session_id="product_scan")`

Pure code. No fluff. Just patterns in action. Because sometimes, you just need to see how it's done.

Why This Matters (Especially for Smaller LLMs)
----------------------------------------------

Here's the thing about AI assistants: the smaller ones can't think their way out of a paper bag. They're like eager interns—full of potential but needing clear guidance. When you rely on a large language model to "figure it out" from raw API docs, you're asking it to reinvent your library's philosophy from scratch. Every. Single. Time.

By providing structured context across these three dimensions, we're not just documenting—we're teaching. We're transferring not just knowledge, but wisdom.

The Cultural DNA of Your Library
--------------------------------

🧬

**Your library's reasoning is its cultural DNA.**  
It reflects your taste, your architectural decisions, your opinions about how things should be done. A library without reasoning is like a recipe without techniques—sure, you have the ingredients, but good luck making something edible.

Think about it: When you learn a new library, what are you really after? You want mastery. And mastery comes from understanding: - **Memory** tells you what's possible - **Reasoning** tells you what's sensible - **Examples** show you what's practical

Together, they create wisdom.

Beyond Manual Documentation
---------------------------

Now, here's where it gets interesting. I didn't hand-craft thousands of lines of structured documentation for Crawl4AI. Who has that kind of time? Instead, I built a tool that:

1.  Analyzes your codebase
2.  Extracts API signatures and structures (Memory)
3.  Identifies patterns and architectural decisions (Reasoning)
4.  Collects real-world usage from tests and examples (Examples)
5.  Generates structured LLM context files

The beauty? This tool is becoming part of Crawl4AI itself. Because if we're going to revolutionize how AI understands our code, we might as well automate it.

The Protocol, Not the Prescription
----------------------------------

Remember: this is a protocol, not a prescription. Just as HTTP doesn't tell you what website to build, the LLM Context Protocol doesn't dictate your documentation style. It simply says:

> "If you want an AI to truly understand your library, it needs three things: facts, philosophy, and patterns."

How you deliver those is up to you. The protocol just ensures nothing important gets lost in translation.

Try It Yourself
---------------

Curious about implementing this for your own library? The context generation tool will be open-sourced as part of Crawl4AI. If you're interested in early access or want to discuss the approach, drop me a DM on X [@unclecode](https://twitter.com/unclecode)
.

Because let's face it: if we're going to live in a world where AI writes half our code, we might as well teach it properly.

* * *

A Final Thought
---------------

Memory + Reasoning + Examples = Wisdom

And wisdom, not information, is what makes great developers—human or artificial.

* * *

_Want to see this in action? Check out the [Crawl4AI LLM Context Builder](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/llmtxt/)
 and experience the difference structured context makes._

* * *

\> Feedback

---

# Release Summary for Version 0.4.0 (December 1, 2024) - Crawl4AI Documentation (v0.8.x)

Release Summary for Version 0.4.0 (December 1, 2024)
====================================================

Overview
--------

The 0.4.0 release introduces significant improvements to content filtering, multi-threaded environment handling, user-agent generation, and test coverage. Key highlights include the introduction of the PruningContentFilter, designed to automatically identify and extract the most valuable parts of an HTML document, as well as enhancements to the BM25ContentFilter to extend its versatility and effectiveness.

Major Features and Enhancements
-------------------------------

### 1\. PruningContentFilter

*   Introduced a new unsupervised content filtering strategy that scores and prunes less relevant nodes in an HTML document based on metrics like text and link density.
*   Focuses on retaining the most valuable parts of the content, making it highly effective for extracting relevant information from complex web pages.
*   Fully documented with updated README and expanded user guides.

### 2\. User-Agent Generator

*   Added a user-agent generator utility that resolves compatibility issues and supports customizable user-agent strings.
*   By default, the generator randomizes user agents for each request, adding diversity, but users can customize it for tailored scenarios.

### 3\. Enhanced Thread Safety

*   Improved handling of multi-threaded environments by adding better thread locks for parallel processing, ensuring consistency and stability when running multiple threads.

### 4\. Extended Content Filtering Strategies

*   Users now have access to both the PruningContentFilter for unsupervised extraction and the BM25ContentFilter for supervised filtering based on user queries.
*   Enhanced BM25ContentFilter with improved capabilities to process page titles, meta tags, and descriptions, allowing for more effective classification and clustering of text chunks.

### 5\. Documentation Updates

*   Updated examples and tutorials to promote the use of the PruningContentFilter alongside the BM25ContentFilter, providing clear instructions for selecting the appropriate filter for each use case.

### 6\. Unit Test Enhancements

*   Added unit tests for PruningContentFilter to ensure accuracy and reliability.
*   Enhanced BM25ContentFilter tests to cover additional edge cases and performance metrics, particularly for malformed HTML inputs.

Revised Change Logs for Version 0.4.0
-------------------------------------

### PruningContentFilter (Dec 01, 2024)

*   Introduced the PruningContentFilter to optimize content extraction by pruning less relevant HTML nodes.
*   **Affected Files:**
    *   **crawl4ai/content\_filter\_strategy.py**: Added a scoring-based pruning algorithm.
    *   **README.md**: Updated to include PruningContentFilter usage.
    *   **docs/md\_v2/basic/content\_filtering.md**: Expanded user documentation, detailing the use and benefits of PruningContentFilter.

### Unit Tests for PruningContentFilter (Dec 01, 2024)

*   Added comprehensive unit tests for PruningContentFilter to ensure correctness and efficiency.
*   **Affected Files:**
    *   **tests/async/test\_content\_filter\_prune.py**: Created tests covering different pruning scenarios to ensure stability and correctness.

### Enhanced BM25ContentFilter Tests (Dec 01, 2024)

*   Expanded tests to cover additional extraction scenarios and performance metrics, improving robustness.
*   **Affected Files:**
    *   **tests/async/test\_content\_filter\_bm25.py**: Added tests for edge cases, including malformed HTML inputs.

### Documentation and Example Updates (Dec 01, 2024)

*   Revised examples to illustrate the use of PruningContentFilter alongside existing content filtering methods.
*   **Affected Files:**
    *   **docs/examples/quickstart\_async.py**: Enhanced example clarity and usability for new users.

Experimental Features
---------------------

*   The PruningContentFilter is still under experimental development, and we continue to gather feedback for further refinements.

Conclusion
----------

This release significantly enhances the content extraction capabilities of Crawl4ai with the introduction of the PruningContentFilter, improved supervised filtering with BM25ContentFilter, and robust multi-threaded handling. Additionally, the user-agent generator provides much-needed versatility, resolving compatibility issues faced by many users.

Users are encouraged to experiment with the new content filtering methods to determine which best suits their needs.

* * *

\> Feedback

---

# 0.4.2 - Crawl4AI Documentation (v0.8.x)

🚀 Crawl4AI 0.4.2 Update: Smarter Crawling Just Got Easier (Dec 12, 2024)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Hey Developers,

I’m excited to share Crawl4AI 0.4.2—a major upgrade that makes crawling smarter, faster, and a whole lot more intuitive. I’ve packed in a bunch of new features to simplify your workflows and improve your experience. Let’s cut to the chase!

* * *

### 🔧 **Configurable Browser and Crawler Behavior**

You’ve asked for better control over how browsers and crawlers are configured, and now you’ve got it. With the new `BrowserConfig` and `CrawlerRunConfig` objects, you can set up your browser and crawling behavior exactly how you want. No more cluttering `arun` with a dozen arguments—just pass in your configs and go.

**Example:**

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-0-1) from crawl4ai import BrowserConfig, CrawlerRunConfig, AsyncWebCrawler [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-0-2) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-0-3) browser_config = BrowserConfig(headless=True, viewport_width=1920, viewport_height=1080) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-0-4) crawler_config = CrawlerRunConfig(cache_mode="BYPASS") [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-0-5) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-0-6) async with AsyncWebCrawler(config=browser_config) as crawler: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-0-7)     result = await crawler.arun(url="https://example.com", config=crawler_config) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-0-8)     print(result.markdown[:500])`

This setup is a game-changer for scalability, keeping your code clean and flexible as we add more parameters in the future.

Remember: If you like to use the old way, you can still pass arguments directly to `arun` as before, no worries!

* * *

### 🔐 **Streamlined Session Management**

Here’s the big one: You can now pass local storage and cookies directly. Whether it’s setting values programmatically or importing a saved JSON state, managing sessions has never been easier. This is a must-have for authenticated crawls—just export your storage state once and reuse it effortlessly across runs.

**Example:** 1. Open a browser, log in manually, and export the storage state. 2. Import the JSON file for seamless authenticated crawling:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-1-1) result = await crawler.arun( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-1-2)     url="https://example.com/protected", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-1-3)     storage_state="my_storage_state.json" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.2/#__codelineno-1-4) )`

* * *

### 🔢 **Handling Large Pages: Supercharged Screenshots and PDF Conversion**

Two big upgrades here:

*   **Blazing-fast long-page screenshots**: Turn extremely long web pages into clean, high-quality screenshots—without breaking a sweat. It’s optimized to handle large content without lag.
    
*   **Full-page PDF exports**: Now, you can also convert any page into a PDF with all the details intact. Perfect for archiving or sharing complex layouts.
    

* * *

### 🔧 **Other Cool Stuff**

*   **Anti-bot enhancements**: Magic mode now handles overlays, user simulation, and anti-detection features like a pro.
*   **JavaScript execution**: Execute custom JS snippets to handle dynamic content. No more wrestling with endless page interactions.

* * *

### 📊 **Performance Boosts and Dev-friendly Updates**

*   Faster rendering and viewport adjustments for better performance.
*   Improved cookie and local storage handling for seamless authentication.
*   Better debugging with detailed logs and actionable error messages.

* * *

### 🔠 **Use Cases You’ll Love**

1. **Authenticated Crawls**: Login once, export your storage state, and reuse it across multiple requests without the headache. 2. **Long-page Screenshots**: Perfect for blogs, e-commerce pages, or any endless-scroll website. 3. **PDF Export**: Create professional-looking page PDFs in seconds.

* * *

### Let’s Get Crawling

Crawl4AI 0.4.2 is ready for you to download and try. I’m always looking for ways to improve, so don’t hold back—share your thoughts and feedback.

Happy Crawling! 🚀

* * *

\> Feedback

---

# Release Summary for Version 0.4.1 (December 8, 2024): Major Efficiency Boosts with New Features! - Crawl4AI Documentation (v0.8.x)

Release Summary for Version 0.4.1 (December 8, 2024): Major Efficiency Boosts with New Features!
================================================================================================

_This post was generated with the help of ChatGPT, take everything with a grain of salt. 🧂_

Hi everyone,

I just finished putting together version 0.4.1 of Crawl4AI, and there are a few changes in here that I think you’ll find really helpful. I’ll explain what’s new, why it matters, and exactly how you can use these features (with the code to back it up). Let’s get into it.

* * *

### Handling Lazy Loading Better (Images Included)

One thing that always bugged me with crawlers is how often they miss lazy-loaded content, especially images. In this version, I made sure Crawl4AI **waits for all images to load** before moving forward. This is useful because many modern websites only load images when they’re in the viewport or after some JavaScript executes.

Here’s how to enable it:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-0-1) await crawler.crawl( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-0-2)     url="https://example.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-0-3)     wait_for_images=True  # Add this argument to ensure images are fully loaded [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-0-4) )`

What this does is: 1. Waits for the page to reach a "network idle" state. 2. Ensures all images on the page have been completely loaded.

This single change handles the majority of lazy-loading cases you’re likely to encounter.

* * *

### Text-Only Mode (Fast, Lightweight Crawling)

Sometimes, you don’t need to download images or process JavaScript at all. For example, if you’re crawling to extract text data, you can enable **text-only mode** to speed things up. By disabling images, JavaScript, and other heavy resources, this mode makes crawling **3-4 times faster** in most cases.

Here’s how to turn it on:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-1-1) crawler = AsyncPlaywrightCrawlerStrategy( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-1-2)     text_mode=True  # Set this to True to enable text-only crawling [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-1-3) )`

When `text_mode=True`, the crawler automatically: - Disables GPU processing. - Blocks image and JavaScript resources. - Reduces the viewport size to 800x600 (you can override this with `viewport_width` and `viewport_height`).

If you need to crawl thousands of pages where you only care about text, this mode will save you a ton of time and resources.

* * *

### Adjusting the Viewport Dynamically

Another useful addition is the ability to **dynamically adjust the viewport size** to match the content on the page. This is particularly helpful when you’re working with responsive layouts or want to ensure all parts of the page load properly.

Here’s how it works: 1. The crawler calculates the page’s width and height after it loads. 2. It adjusts the viewport to fit the content dimensions. 3. (Optional) It uses Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) to simulate zooming out so everything fits in the viewport.

To enable this, use:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-2-1) await crawler.crawl( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-2-2)     url="https://example.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-2-3)     adjust_viewport_to_content=True  # Dynamically adjusts the viewport [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-2-4) )`

This approach makes sure the entire page gets loaded into the viewport, especially for layouts that load content based on visibility.

* * *

### Simulating Full-Page Scrolling

Some websites load data dynamically as you scroll down the page. To handle these cases, I added support for **full-page scanning**. It simulates scrolling to the bottom of the page, checking for new content, and capturing it all.

Here’s an example:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-3-1) await crawler.crawl( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-3-2)     url="https://example.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-3-3)     scan_full_page=True,   # Enables scrolling [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-3-4)     scroll_delay=0.2       # Waits 200ms between scrolls (optional) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-3-5) )`

What happens here: 1. The crawler scrolls down in increments, waiting for content to load after each scroll. 2. It stops when no new content appears (i.e., dynamic elements stop loading). 3. It scrolls back to the top before finishing (if necessary).

If you’ve ever had to deal with infinite scroll pages, this is going to save you a lot of headaches.

* * *

### Reusing Browser Sessions (Save Time on Setup)

By default, every time you crawl a page, a new browser context (or tab) is created. That’s fine for small crawls, but if you’re working on a large dataset, it’s more efficient to reuse the same session.

I added a method called `create_session` for this:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-1) session_id = await crawler.create_session() [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-2) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-3) # Use the same session for multiple crawls [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-4) await crawler.crawl( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-5)     url="https://example.com/page1", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-6)     session_id=session_id  # Reuse the session [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-7) ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-8) await crawler.crawl( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-9)     url="https://example.com/page2", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-10)     session_id=session_id [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-4-11) )`

This avoids creating a new tab for every page, speeding up the crawl and reducing memory usage.

* * *

### Other Updates

Here are a few smaller updates I’ve made: - **Light Mode**: Use `light_mode=True` to disable background processes, extensions, and other unnecessary features, making the browser more efficient. - **Logging**: Improved logs to make debugging easier. - **Defaults**: Added sensible defaults for things like `delay_before_return_html` (now set to 0.1 seconds).

* * *

### How to Get the Update

You can install or upgrade to version `0.4.1` like this:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.4.1/#__codelineno-5-1) pip install crawl4ai --upgrade`

As always, I’d love to hear your thoughts. If there’s something you think could be improved or if you have suggestions for future versions, let me know!

Enjoy the new features, and happy crawling! 🕷️

* * *

* * *

\> Feedback

---

# Crawl4AI v0.6.0 Release Notes - Crawl4AI Documentation (v0.8.x)

Crawl4AI v0.6.0 Release Notes
=============================

We're excited to announce the release of **Crawl4AI v0.6.0**, our biggest and most feature-rich update yet. This version introduces major architectural upgrades, brand-new capabilities for geo-aware crawling, high-efficiency scraping, and real-time streaming support for scalable deployments.

* * *

Highlights
----------

### 1\. **World-Aware Crawlers**

Crawl as if you’re anywhere in the world. With v0.6.0, each crawl can simulate: - Specific GPS coordinates - Browser locale - Timezone

Example:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-0-1) CrawlerRunConfig( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-0-2)     url="https://browserleaks.com/geo", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-0-3)     locale="en-US", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-0-4)     timezone_id="America/Los_Angeles", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-0-5)     geolocation=GeolocationConfig( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-0-6)         latitude=34.0522, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-0-7)         longitude=-118.2437, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-0-8)         accuracy=10.0 [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-0-9)     ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-0-10) )`

Great for accessing region-specific content or testing global behavior.

* * *

### 2\. **Native Table Extraction**

Extract HTML tables directly into usable formats like Pandas DataFrames or CSV with zero parsing hassle. All table data is available under `result.media["tables"]`.

Example:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-1-1) raw_df = pd.DataFrame( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-1-2)     result.media["tables"][0]["rows"], [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-1-3)     columns=result.media["tables"][0]["headers"] [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-1-4) )`

This makes it ideal for scraping financial data, pricing pages, or anything tabular.

* * *

### 3\. **Browser Pooling & Pre-Warming**

We've overhauled browser management. Now, multiple browser instances can be pooled and pages pre-warmed for ultra-fast launches: - Reduces cold-start latency - Lowers memory spikes - Enhances parallel crawling stability

This powers the new **Docker Playground** experience and streamlines heavy-load crawling.

* * *

### 4\. **Traffic & Snapshot Capture**

Need full visibility? You can now capture: - Full network traffic logs - Console output - MHTML page snapshots for post-crawl audits and debugging

No more guesswork on what happened during your crawl.

* * *

### 5\. **MCP API and Streaming Support**

We’re exposing **MCP socket and SSE endpoints**, allowing: - Live streaming of crawl results - Real-time integration with agents or frontends - A new Playground UI for interactive crawling

This is a major step towards making Crawl4AI real-time ready.

* * *

### 6\. **Stress-Test Framework**

Want to test performance under heavy load? v0.6.0 includes a new memory stress-test suite that supports 1,000+ URL workloads. Ideal for: - Load testing - Performance benchmarking - Validating memory efficiency

* * *

Core Improvements
-----------------

*   Robots.txt compliance
*   Proxy rotation support
*   Improved URL normalization and session reuse
*   Shared data across crawler hooks
*   New page routing logic

* * *

Breaking Changes & Deprecations
-------------------------------

*   Legacy `crawl4ai/browser/*` modules are removed. Update imports accordingly.
*   `AsyncPlaywrightCrawlerStrategy.get_page` now uses a new function signature.
*   Deprecated markdown generator aliases now point to `DefaultMarkdownGenerator` with warning.

* * *

Miscellaneous Updates
---------------------

*   FastAPI validators replaced custom validation logic
*   Docker build now based on a Chromium layer
*   Repo-wide cleanup: ~36,000 insertions, ~5,000 deletions

* * *

New Examples Included
---------------------

*   Geo-location crawling
*   Network + console log capture
*   Docker MCP API usage
*   Markdown selector usage
*   Crypto project data extraction

* * *

Watch the Release Video
-----------------------

Want a visual walkthrough of all these updates? Watch the video: 🔗 https://youtu.be/9x7nVcjOZks

If you're new to Crawl4AI, start here: 🔗 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xo3qK6Hg9AA&t=15s

* * *

Join the Community
------------------

We’ve just opened up our **Discord** for the public. Join us to: - Ask questions - Share your projects - Get help or contribute

💬 https://discord.gg/wpYFACrHR4

* * *

Install or Upgrade
------------------

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.6.0/#__codelineno-2-1) pip install -U crawl4ai`

* * *

Live long and import crawl4ai. 🖖

* * *

\> Feedback

---

-----------------

# Cache Modes - Crawl4AI Documentation (v0.8.x)

Crawl4AI Cache System and Migration Guide
=========================================

Overview
--------

Starting from version 0.5.0, Crawl4AI introduces a new caching system that replaces the old boolean flags with a more intuitive `CacheMode` enum. This change simplifies cache control and makes the behavior more predictable.

Old vs New Approach
-------------------

### Old Way (Deprecated)

The old system used multiple boolean flags: - `bypass_cache`: Skip cache entirely - `disable_cache`: Disable all caching - `no_cache_read`: Don't read from cache - `no_cache_write`: Don't write to cache

### New Way (Recommended)

The new system uses a single `CacheMode` enum: - `CacheMode.ENABLED`: Normal caching (read/write) - `CacheMode.DISABLED`: No caching at all - `CacheMode.READ_ONLY`: Only read from cache - `CacheMode.WRITE_ONLY`: Only write to cache - `CacheMode.BYPASS`: Skip cache for this operation

Migration Example
-----------------

### Old Code (Deprecated)

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-1) import asyncio [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-2) from crawl4ai import AsyncWebCrawler [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-3) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-4) async def use_proxy(): [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-5)     async with AsyncWebCrawler(verbose=True) as crawler: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-6)         result = await crawler.arun( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-7)             url="https://www.nbcnews.com/business", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-8)             bypass_cache=True  # Old way [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-9)         ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-10)         print(len(result.markdown)) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-11) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-12) async def main(): [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-13)     await use_proxy() [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-14) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-15) if __name__ == "__main__": [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-0-16)     asyncio.run(main())`

### New Code (Recommended)

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-1) import asyncio [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-2) from crawl4ai import AsyncWebCrawler, CacheMode [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-3) from crawl4ai.async_configs import CrawlerRunConfig [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-4) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-5) async def use_proxy(): [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-6)     # Use CacheMode in CrawlerRunConfig [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-7)     config = CrawlerRunConfig(cache_mode=CacheMode.BYPASS)   [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-8)     async with AsyncWebCrawler(verbose=True) as crawler: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-9)         result = await crawler.arun( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-10)             url="https://www.nbcnews.com/business", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-11)             config=config  # Pass the configuration object [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-12)         ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-13)         print(len(result.markdown)) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-14) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-15) async def main(): [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-16)     await use_proxy() [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-17) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-18) if __name__ == "__main__": [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/core/cache-modes/#__codelineno-1-19)     asyncio.run(main())`

Common Migration Patterns
-------------------------

| Old Flag | New Mode |
| --- | --- |
| `bypass_cache=True` | `cache_mode=CacheMode.BYPASS` |
| `disable_cache=True` | `cache_mode=CacheMode.DISABLED` |
| `no_cache_read=True` | `cache_mode=CacheMode.WRITE_ONLY` |
| `no_cache_write=True` | `cache_mode=CacheMode.READ_ONLY` |

* * *

\> Feedback

---

# 🚀 Crawl4AI v0.7.2: CI/CD & Dependency Optimization Update - Crawl4AI Documentation (v0.8.x)

🚀 Crawl4AI v0.7.2: CI/CD & Dependency Optimization Update
==========================================================

_July 25, 2025 • 3 min read_

* * *

This release introduces automated CI/CD pipelines for seamless releases and optimizes dependencies for a lighter, more efficient package.

🎯 What's New
-------------

### 🔄 Automated Release Pipeline

*   **GitHub Actions CI/CD**: Automated PyPI and Docker Hub releases on tag push
*   **Multi-platform Docker images**: Support for both AMD64 and ARM64 architectures
*   **Version consistency checks**: Ensures tag, package, and Docker versions align
*   **Automated release notes**: GitHub releases created automatically

### 📦 Dependency Optimization

*   **Moved sentence-transformers to optional dependencies**: Significantly reduces default installation size
*   **Lighter Docker images**: Optimized Dockerfile for faster builds and smaller images
*   **Better dependency management**: Core vs. optional dependencies clearly separated

🏗️ CI/CD Pipeline
------------------

The new automated release process ensures consistent, reliable releases:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-0-1) # Trigger releases with a simple tag [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-0-2) git tag v0.7.2 [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-0-3) git push origin v0.7.2 [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-0-4) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-0-5) # Automatically: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-0-6) # ✅ Validates version consistency [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-0-7) # ✅ Builds and publishes to PyPI [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-0-8) # ✅ Builds multi-platform Docker images [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-0-9) # ✅ Pushes to Docker Hub with proper tags [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-0-10) # ✅ Creates GitHub release`

💾 Lighter Installation
-----------------------

Default installation is now significantly smaller:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-1-1) # Core installation (smaller, faster) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-1-2) pip install crawl4ai==0.7.2 [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-1-3) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-1-4) # With ML features (includes sentence-transformers) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-1-5) pip install crawl4ai[transformer]==0.7.2 [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-1-6) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-1-7) # Full installation [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-1-8) pip install crawl4ai[all]==0.7.2`

🐳 Docker Improvements
----------------------

Enhanced Docker support with multi-platform images:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-2-1) # Pull the latest version [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-2-2) docker pull unclecode/crawl4ai:0.7.2 [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-2-3) docker pull unclecode/crawl4ai:latest [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-2-4) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-2-5) # Available tags: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-2-6) # - unclecode/crawl4ai:0.7.2 (specific version) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-2-7) # - unclecode/crawl4ai:0.7 (minor version) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-2-8) # - unclecode/crawl4ai:0 (major version) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-2-9) # - unclecode/crawl4ai:latest`

🔧 Technical Details
--------------------

### Dependency Changes

*   `sentence-transformers` moved from required to optional dependencies
*   Reduces default installation by ~500MB
*   No impact on functionality when transformer features aren't needed

### CI/CD Configuration

*   GitHub Actions workflows for automated releases
*   Version validation before publishing
*   Parallel PyPI and Docker Hub deployments
*   Automatic tagging strategy for Docker images

🚀 Installation
---------------

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.2/#__codelineno-3-1) pip install crawl4ai==0.7.2`

No breaking changes - direct upgrade from v0.7.0 or v0.7.1.

* * *

Questions? Issues? - GitHub: [github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai](https://github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai)
 - Discord: [discord.gg/crawl4ai](https://discord.gg/jP8KfhDhyN)
 - Twitter: [@unclecode](https://x.com/unclecode)

_P.S. The new CI/CD pipeline will make future releases faster and more reliable. Thanks for your patience as we improve our release process!_

* * *

\> Feedback

---

# Crawl4AI 0.4.3: Major Performance Boost & LLM Integration - Crawl4AI Documentation (v0.8.x)

Crawl4AI 0.4.3: Major Performance Boost & LLM Integration
=========================================================

We're excited to announce Crawl4AI 0.4.3, focusing on three key areas: Speed & Efficiency, LLM Integration, and Core Platform Improvements. This release significantly improves crawling performance while adding powerful new LLM-powered features.

⚡ Speed & Efficiency Improvements
---------------------------------

### 1\. Memory-Adaptive Dispatcher System

The new dispatcher system provides intelligent resource management and real-time monitoring:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-1) from crawl4ai import AsyncWebCrawler, CrawlerRunConfig, DisplayMode [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-2) from crawl4ai.async_dispatcher import MemoryAdaptiveDispatcher, CrawlerMonitor [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-3) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-4) async def main(): [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-5)     urls = ["https://example1.com", "https://example2.com"] * 50 [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-6) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-7)     # Configure memory-aware dispatch [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-8)     dispatcher = MemoryAdaptiveDispatcher( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-9)         memory_threshold_percent=80.0,  # Auto-throttle at 80% memory [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-10)         check_interval=0.5,             # Check every 0.5 seconds [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-11)         max_session_permit=20,          # Max concurrent sessions [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-12)         monitor=CrawlerMonitor(         # Real-time monitoring [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-13)             display_mode=DisplayMode.DETAILED [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-14)         ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-15)     ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-16) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-17)     async with AsyncWebCrawler() as crawler: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-18)         results = await dispatcher.run_urls( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-19)             urls=urls, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-20)             crawler=crawler, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-21)             config=CrawlerRunConfig() [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-0-22)         )`

### 2\. Streaming Support

Process crawled URLs in real-time instead of waiting for all results:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-1-1) config = CrawlerRunConfig(stream=True) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-1-2) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-1-3) async with AsyncWebCrawler() as crawler: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-1-4)     async for result in await crawler.arun_many(urls, config=config): [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-1-5)         print(f"Got result for {result.url}") [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-1-6)         # Process each result immediately`

### 3\. LXML-Based Scraping

New LXML scraping strategy offering up to 20x faster parsing:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-2-1) config = CrawlerRunConfig( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-2-2)     scraping_strategy=LXMLWebScrapingStrategy(), [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-2-3)     cache_mode=CacheMode.ENABLED [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-2-4) )`

🤖 LLM Integration
------------------

### 1\. LLM-Powered Markdown Generation

Smart content filtering and organization using LLMs:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-3-1) config = CrawlerRunConfig( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-3-2)     markdown_generator=DefaultMarkdownGenerator( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-3-3)         content_filter=LLMContentFilter( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-3-4)             provider="openai/gpt-4o", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-3-5)             instruction="Extract technical documentation and code examples" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-3-6)         ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-3-7)     ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-3-8) )`

### 2\. Automatic Schema Generation

Generate extraction schemas instantly using LLMs instead of manual CSS/XPath writing:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-4-1) schema = JsonCssExtractionStrategy.generate_schema( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-4-2)     html_content, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-4-3)     schema_type="CSS", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-4-4)     query="Extract product name, price, and description" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-4-5) )`

🔧 Core Improvements
--------------------

### 1\. Proxy Support & Rotation

Integrated proxy support with automatic rotation and verification:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-5-1) config = CrawlerRunConfig( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-5-2)     proxy_config={ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-5-3)         "server": "http://proxy:8080", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-5-4)         "username": "user", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-5-5)         "password": "pass" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-5-6)     } [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-5-7) )`

### 2\. Robots.txt Compliance

Built-in robots.txt support with SQLite caching:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-6-1) config = CrawlerRunConfig(check_robots_txt=True) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-6-2) result = await crawler.arun(url, config=config) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-6-3) if result.status_code == 403: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-6-4)     print("Access blocked by robots.txt")`

### 3\. URL Redirection Tracking

Track final URLs after redirects:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-7-1) result = await crawler.arun(url) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-7-2) print(f"Initial URL: {url}") [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-7-3) print(f"Final URL: {result.redirected_url}")`

Performance Impact
------------------

*   Memory usage reduced by up to 40% with adaptive dispatcher
*   Parsing speed increased up to 20x with LXML strategy
*   Streaming reduces memory footprint for large crawls by ~60%

Getting Started
---------------

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/v0.4.3b1/#__codelineno-8-1) pip install -U crawl4ai`

For complete examples, check our [demo repository](https://github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai/examples)
.

Stay Connected
--------------

*   Star us on [GitHub](https://github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai)
    
*   Follow [@unclecode](https://twitter.com/unclecode)
    
*   Join our [Discord](https://discord.gg/crawl4ai)
    

Happy crawling! 🕷️

* * *

\> Feedback

# 🚀 Crawl4AI v0.7.3: The Multi-Config Intelligence Update - Crawl4AI Documentation (v0.8.x)

🚀 Crawl4AI v0.7.3: The Multi-Config Intelligence Update
========================================================

_August 6, 2025 • 5 min read_

* * *

Today I'm releasing Crawl4AI v0.7.3—the Multi-Config Intelligence Update. This release brings smarter URL-specific configurations, flexible Docker deployments, important bug fixes, and documentation improvements that make Crawl4AI more robust and production-ready.

🎯 What's New at a Glance
-------------------------

*   **Multi-URL Configurations**: Different crawling strategies for different URL patterns in a single batch
*   **Flexible Docker LLM Providers**: Configure LLM providers via environment variables
*   **Bug Fixes**: Resolved several critical issues for better stability
*   **Documentation Updates**: Clearer examples and improved API documentation

🎨 Multi-URL Configurations: One Size Doesn't Fit All
-----------------------------------------------------

**The Problem:** You're crawling a mix of documentation sites, blogs, and API endpoints. Each needs different handling—caching for docs, fresh content for news, structured extraction for APIs. Previously, you'd run separate crawls or write complex conditional logic.

**My Solution:** I implemented URL-specific configurations that let you define different strategies for different URL patterns in a single crawl batch. First match wins, with optional fallback support.

### Technical Implementation

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-1) from crawl4ai import AsyncWebCrawler, CrawlerRunConfig, MatchMode [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-2) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-3) # Define specialized configs for different content types [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-4) configs = [ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-5)     # Documentation sites - aggressive caching, include links [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-6)     CrawlerRunConfig( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-7)         url_matcher=["*docs*", "*documentation*"], [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-8)         cache_mode="write", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-9)         markdown_generator_options={"include_links": True} [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-10)     ), [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-11) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-12)     # News/blog sites - fresh content, scroll for lazy loading [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-13)     CrawlerRunConfig( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-14)         url_matcher=lambda url: 'blog' in url or 'news' in url, [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-15)         cache_mode="bypass", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-16)         js_code="window.scrollTo(0, document.body.scrollHeight/2);" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-17)     ), [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-18) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-19)     # API endpoints - structured extraction [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-20)     CrawlerRunConfig( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-21)         url_matcher=["*.json", "*api*"], [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-22)         extraction_strategy=LLMExtractionStrategy( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-23)             provider="openai/gpt-4o-mini", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-24)             extraction_type="structured" [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-25)         ) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-26)     ), [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-27) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-28)     # Default fallback for everything else [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-29)     CrawlerRunConfig()  # No url_matcher = matches everything [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-30) ] [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-31) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-32) # Crawl multiple URLs with appropriate configs [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-33) async with AsyncWebCrawler() as crawler: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-34)     results = await crawler.arun_many( [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-35)         urls=[ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-36)             "https://docs.python.org/3/",      # → Uses documentation config [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-37)             "https://blog.python.org/",        # → Uses blog config   [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-38)             "https://api.github.com/users",    # → Uses API config [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-39)             "https://example.com/"             # → Uses default config [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-40)         ], [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-41)         config=configs [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-0-42)     )`

**Matching Capabilities:** - **String Patterns**: Wildcards like `"*.pdf"`, `"*/blog/*"` - **Function Matchers**: Lambda functions for complex logic - **Mixed Matchers**: Combine strings and functions with AND/OR logic - **Fallback Support**: Default config when nothing matches

**Expected Real-World Impact:** - **Mixed Content Sites**: Handle blogs, docs, and downloads in one crawl - **Multi-Domain Crawling**: Different strategies per domain without separate runs - **Reduced Complexity**: No more if/else forests in your extraction code - **Better Performance**: Each URL gets exactly the processing it needs

🐳 Docker: Flexible LLM Provider Configuration
----------------------------------------------

**The Problem:** Hardcoded LLM providers in Docker deployments. Want to switch from OpenAI to Groq? Rebuild and redeploy. Testing different models? Multiple Docker images.

**My Solution:** Configure LLM providers via environment variables. Switch providers without touching code or rebuilding images.

### Deployment Flexibility

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-1) # Option 1: Direct environment variables [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-2) docker run -d \ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-3)   -e LLM_PROVIDER="groq/llama-3.2-3b-preview" \ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-4)   -e GROQ_API_KEY="your-key" \ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-5)   -p 11235:11235 \ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-6)   unclecode/crawl4ai:latest [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-7) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-8) # Option 2: Using .llm.env file (recommended for production) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-9) # Create .llm.env file: [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-10) # LLM_PROVIDER=openai/gpt-4o-mini [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-11) # OPENAI_API_KEY=your-openai-key [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-12) # GROQ_API_KEY=your-groq-key [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-13) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-14) docker run -d \ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-15)   --env-file .llm.env \ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-16)   -p 11235:11235 \ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-1-17)   unclecode/crawl4ai:latest`

Override per request when needed:

`[](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-1) # Use default provider from .llm.env [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-2) response = requests.post("http://localhost:11235/crawl", json={ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-3)     "url": "https://example.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-4)     "extraction_strategy": {"type": "llm"} [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-5) }) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-6) [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-7) # Override to use different provider for this specific request [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-8) response = requests.post("http://localhost:11235/crawl", json={ [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-9)     "url": "https://complex-page.com", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-10)     "extraction_strategy": { [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-11)         "type": "llm", [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-12)         "provider": "openai/gpt-4"  # Override default [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-13)     } [](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/blog/releases/0.7.3/#__codelineno-2-14) })`

**Expected Real-World Impact:** - **Cost Optimization**: Use cheaper models for simple tasks, premium for complex - **A/B Testing**: Compare provider performance without deployment changes - **Fallback Strategies**: Switch providers on-the-fly during outages - **Development Flexibility**: Test locally with one provider, deploy with another - **Secure Configuration**: Keep API keys in `.llm.env` file, not in commands

🔧 Bug Fixes & Improvements
---------------------------

This release includes several important bug fixes that improve stability and reliability:

*   **URL Matcher Fallback**: Fixed edge cases in URL pattern matching logic
*   **Memory Management**: Resolved memory leaks in long-running crawl sessions
*   **Sitemap Processing**: Fixed redirect handling in sitemap fetching
*   **Table Extraction**: Improved table detection and extraction accuracy
*   **Error Handling**: Better error messages and recovery from network failures

📚 Documentation Enhancements
-----------------------------

Based on community feedback, we've updated: - Clearer examples for multi-URL configuration - Improved CrawlResult documentation with all available fields - Fixed typos and inconsistencies across documentation - Added real-world URLs in examples for better understanding - New comprehensive demo showcasing all v0.7.3 features

🙏 Acknowledgments
------------------

Thanks to our contributors and the entire community for feedback and bug reports.

📚 Resources
------------

*   [Full Documentation](https://docs.crawl4ai.com/)
    
*   [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai)
    
*   [Discord Community](https://discord.gg/crawl4ai)
    
*   [Feature Demo](https://github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai/blob/main/docs/releases_review/demo_v0.7.3.py)
    

* * *

_Crawl4AI continues to evolve with your needs. This release makes it smarter, more flexible, and more stable. Try the new multi-config feature and flexible Docker deployment—they're game changers!_

**Happy Crawling! 🕷️**

_\- The Crawl4AI Team_

* * *

\> Feedback
